content.problemStatement:
This semester, Chef took XXX courses. Each course has YYY units and each unit has ZZZ chapters in it.

Find the total number of chapters Chef has to study this semester.


Input Format

The first line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case consists of a single line of input, containing three space-separated integers X,Y,X, Y,X,Y, and ZZZ.


Output Format
For each test case, output in a single line the total number of chapters Chef has to study this semester.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains three space‑separated integers X, Y and Z denoting respectively the number of courses, units per course and chapters per unit.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the total number of chapters, i.e. X·Y·Z.

solution.approach: For each test case we just need the product of the three given integers. The algorithm is straightforward: read X, Y, Z and print X*Y*Z. Multiplication of three numbers fits in 32‑bit signed integer because the maximum value is 1000³ = 1,000,000,000 which is < 2³¹. The algorithm runs in O(T) time and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – one constant‑time multiplication per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables are used.

code.Python3:
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    t = int(data[0])
    out = []
    idx = 1
    for _ in range(t):
        x = int(data[idx]); y = int(data[idx+1]); z = int(data[idx+2])
        out.append(str(x*y*z))
        idx += 3
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
